title: AI Pair Programming — The Copilot Awakens

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you'll set up GitHub Copilot and experience inline completions and Copilot Chat first-hand.

The guide panel will update automatically as you advance through the slides. The glowing edge and arrow will show you where to look.

steps.title: Install the Extension
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Extensions panel for you (Ctrl+Shift+X). Search for GitHub Copilot — it should already be installed, but if it isn't you can install it from here.

A single extension now bundles both inline completions and Copilot Chat.

steps.title: Check the Extension Details
steps.instruction:
Click on the GitHub Copilot extension in the list to open its details page. Scroll through to see what's included:

• Inline completions (ghost text)
• Copilot Chat panel and inline chat
• Terminal integration

Notice the Disable and Uninstall buttons — you won't need those today, but it's good to know where they are.

steps.title: Verify the Status Bar
steps.instruction:
Look at the bottom-right corner of VS Code. You should see a small Copilot icon (a sparkle) in the status bar.

If it's visible and not greyed out, the extension is active. Click it to see quick actions like signing in, checking status, or disabling Copilot temporarily.

steps.title: Sign In to GitHub
steps.instruction:
Click the Copilot icon in the status bar → Sign in to GitHub. Authorize VS Code in your browser, then come back here.

The icon should turn into the Copilot sparkle when you're active.

steps.title: Verify Your License
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) and type:

GitHub Copilot: Check Status

This confirms whether Copilot is connected and your license is active. If it reports a problem, check your organization seat assignment or try signing out and back in.

steps.title: Check the Output Logs
steps.instruction:
Open the Output panel (Ctrl+Shift+U) and select GitHub Copilot from the dropdown.

This is where Copilot logs connection events and errors. If you ever run into trouble, this panel is your first stop for diagnosing issues.
steps.tip: Bookmark this in your mind — the Output panel is the single best debugging tool for Copilot connectivity problems.

steps.title: Try Inline Completions
steps.instruction:
We've opened a new file for you. Set the language to JavaScript using the language selector in the bottom-right corner, then type this comment:

// Calculate the total price of items in a shopping cart

Press Enter and watch Copilot suggest the function body as ghost text. Press Tab to accept the full suggestion.

steps.title: Accept Word-by-Word
steps.instruction:
Now try a different example. Below your first function, type:

// Validate an email address and return true or false

When the ghost text appears, instead of pressing Tab, press Ctrl+→ repeatedly. Each press accepts one word at a time — perfect when the suggestion is mostly right but you want to tweak as you go.
steps.tip: This is the most underused shortcut in Copilot. Once you learn it, you'll use it constantly.

steps.title: Cycle Through Alternatives
steps.instruction:
Try one more. Type:

// Reverse a string without using built-in reverse methods

When ghost text appears, press Alt+] to see the next alternative and Alt+[ for the previous one. Copilot often has multiple approaches — a for-loop, a reduce, a spread-and-reverse. Explore them before accepting.

steps.title: Dismiss and Redirect
steps.instruction:
Type a new comment:

// Shuffle an array randomly

When ghost text appears, press Esc to dismiss it. Now start typing the function signature manually: function shuffle(arr) {

Notice how Copilot generates a different suggestion once you've given it more context. The more structure you provide, the better the result.

steps.title: Open Copilot Chat
steps.instruction: We've opened the Copilot Chat panel for you (Ctrl+Shift+I). You'll see the chat interface on the right side of your screen.

steps.title: Choose Your Model
steps.instruction:
Click the model selector dropdown at the top of the Chat panel. Different models have different strengths — Claude excels at reasoning, GPT-4o is fast and versatile.

For this lab, any model works. Pick one and continue.

steps.title: Ask a Context-Aware Question
steps.instruction:
With the file you wrote earlier still open, type:

How could I improve the functions in my current file?

Notice that Copilot sees your open file and gives specific feedback — not generic advice. This context awareness is what makes Chat so much more useful than a web search.

steps.title: Try Inline Chat
steps.instruction:
Click inside one of your functions in the editor, then press Ctrl+I to open inline chat — a small input box right in the editor.

Type: Add input validation

Inline chat is perfect for quick, targeted edits without switching to the sidebar.
steps.tip: <kbd>Ctrl+Shift+I</kbd> opens the sidebar panel. <kbd>Ctrl+I</kbd> opens inline chat. Two different tools for two different jobs.

steps.title: Explain Your Code
steps.instruction:
Select one of the functions you wrote earlier, then type in the chat input:

/explain

Copilot breaks down how the code works step by step — great for understanding unfamiliar code or verifying your own logic.

steps.title: Fix a Problem
steps.instruction:
Now try /fix on the same selection. Even if your code looks correct, Copilot will often find edge cases — empty arrays, null values, unexpected types.

Review the diff and click Apply if the fix looks good.

steps.title: Generate Tests
steps.instruction:
Select your function and type /tests in the chat. Copilot generates unit tests covering normal cases, edge cases, and error conditions.

Click Insert into New File to save them alongside your code.

steps.title: Ask About Your Workspace
steps.instruction:
Now try the @workspace participant. Type:

@workspace What files are in this project and what do they do?

Unlike regular chat, @workspace indexes your entire project — not just the open file. It can answer questions about project structure, dependencies, and cross-file relationships.
steps.tip: Type <kbd>/</kbd> in the chat input to see all available commands. There are more than the four we covered here.

steps.title: Explore Copilot Settings
steps.instruction:
We've opened Settings for you (Ctrl+,). Search for copilot to see all available options.

Find github.copilot.enable and notice it accepts per-language toggles — you can enable Copilot for Python but disable it for Markdown.

steps.title: Try a Per-Language Toggle
steps.instruction:
Click Edit in settings.json next to the github.copilot.enable setting and add:

"github.copilot.enable": { "*": true, "markdown": false }

This keeps Copilot active everywhere except Markdown files. Teams often disable it for plain text and config files where suggestions aren't helpful.
steps.tip: Put these settings in .vscode/settings.json (not your user settings) and commit the file. Everyone who clones the repo gets the same configuration.

steps.title: Preview Custom Instructions
steps.instruction:
Search for copilot instructions in Settings. You'll see the option to point Copilot at a .github/copilot-instructions.md file.

This file lets you encode team standards ("always use async/await", "prefer named exports") so Copilot follows them automatically. The next course in this track covers custom instructions in depth.

steps.title: Open the Terminal
steps.instruction:
We've opened the integrated terminal for you (Ctrl+`).

Copilot isn't just for code files — it works in the terminal too. Try asking in the Chat panel:

@terminal how do I list all git branches, including remote ones?

steps.title: Try Inline Terminal Help
steps.instruction:
Click inside the terminal and press Ctrl+I to open inline Copilot right in the terminal.

Type: Find all JavaScript files modified in the last 7 days

Copilot will suggest the command. Press Enter to insert it into the terminal — it won't run automatically, so you can review it first.
steps.tip: Inline terminal assistance is especially helpful for complex commands with many flags (think git, docker, or kubectl).

steps.title: Ask for a Multi-Step Workflow
steps.instruction:
In the Chat panel, try a more complex request:

@terminal How do I create a new git branch, make a commit, and push it to origin?

Copilot gives you the full sequence of commands with explanations. This is great for CLI workflows you don't use every day.

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! 🎉 You've activated Copilot, used inline completions with four different acceptance methods (Tab, Ctrl+→, Alt+], and Esc), explored Chat commands (/explain, /fix, /tests, @workspace), used both sidebar and inline chat, worked in the terminal, and configured settings.

You're ready for the next course: Prompts, Instructions, Skills, and Agents.
steps.tip: Head back to the slides on the left for a recap, or close this panel to explore on your own.
